tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.

Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.

Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Search 5 richest people in the world and create Person objects in the database.

Completion criteria:
- 5 Person objects in the database.
- Web search works.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

1. Elon Musk — $839B (Tesla, SpaceX)

4. Jeff Bezos — $224B (Amazon)
5. Mark Zuckerberg — $222B (Meta)

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

Query for objects in ECHO.

Currently, two types of queries are supported:

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>

  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>

  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Completion criteria:
- 5 Person objects in the database.
- Web search works.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

Search 5 richest people in the world and create Person objects in the database.

Completion criteria:
- 5 Person objects in the database.
- Web search works.
<input>{}</input>

content.text:
Based on the search results, the top 5 richest people in the world in 2026 according to Forbes are:

1. Elon Musk - CEO of Tesla, SpaceX, xAI
2. Larry Page - Co-founder of Google/Alphabet
3. Sergey Brin - Co-founder of Google/Alphabet
4. Jeff Bezos - Founder of Amazon
5. Mark Zuckerberg - Co-founder of Meta/Facebook

Let me create Person objects for each of them.

I found the top 5 richest people. According to the Forbes 2026 ranking:

1.
Elon Musk - founder of Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI
Larry Page - Google cofounder, net worth of $257 billion
Sergey Brin, with his $237 billion fortune
Jeff Bezos, with $224 billion
Mark Zuckerberg at $222 billion
Now let me create all 5 Person objects in the database:

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.text:
Based on the Bloomberg Billionaires Index (as of June 3, 2026) and the Forbes 2026 rankings, the top 5 richest people are:

1. Elon Musk - $726B (Bloomberg) / ~$627B (Forbes) - CEO of Tesla, SpaceX, xAI
2. Larry Page - $306B (Bloomberg) / $257B (Forbes) - Google cofounder
3. Larry Ellison - $286B (Bloomberg) - Oracle


4. Sergey Brin - $284B (Bloomberg) / $237B (Forbes) - Google cofounder
5. Jeff Bezos - $271B (Bloomberg) / $224B (Forbes) - Amazon founder

The Bloomberg data from early June 2026 shows a notably different ranking than Forbes, with Ellison placing higher than Brin, while Forbes has Brin ahead of Bezos and includes Mark Zuckerberg at $222B in the top five.

I'll go with the Bloomberg figures since they're the most current, and now I'm creating the Person objects for these five billionaires.

2. **Larry Page** - $306B
3. **Larry Ellison** - $286B
4. **Sergey Brin** - $284B
5. **Jeff Bezos** - $271B

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

Search 5 richest people in the world and create Person objects in the database.

Completion criteria:
- 5 Person objects in the database.
- Web search works.
<input>{}</input>

content.text:
Based on the Bloomberg Billionaires Index (as of June 3, 2026) and the Forbes 2026 rankings, the top 5 richest people are:

1. Elon Musk - $726B (Bloomberg) / ~$627B (Forbes) - CEO of Tesla, SpaceX, xAI
2. Larry Page - $306B (Bloomberg) / $257B (Forbes) - Google cofounder
3. Larry Ellison - $286B (Bloomberg) - Oracle


4. Sergey Brin - $284B (Bloomberg) / $237B (Forbes) - Google cofounder
5. Jeff Bezos - $271B (Bloomberg) / $224B (Forbes) - Amazon founder

The Bloomberg data from early June 2026 shows a notably different ranking than Forbes, with Ellison placing higher than Brin, while Forbes has Brin ahead of Bezos and includes Mark Zuckerberg at $222B in the top five.

I'll go with the Bloomberg figures since they're the most current, and now I'm creating the Person objects for these five billionaires.

2. **Larry Page** - $306B
3. **Larry Ellison** - $286B
4. **Sergey Brin** - $284B
5. **Jeff Bezos** - $271B

Now let me create all 5 Person objects in the database.
